title: Skills for Software Engineer Resume
metaDescription: Technical and soft skills to list on a software engineer resume. Skill clusters, bullet examples, and keywords that get past ATS and recruiters.
intro: A strong software engineer resume balances technical depth with clear evidence of impact and collaboration. This guide groups skills into clusters and shows how to turn them into compelling resume bullets that pass ATS and resonate with hiring managers.
bulletExamples: Designed and shipped a new API used by 5 internal services; reduced p99 latency by 30% and improved error handling.
Led migration of legacy monolith to microservices; cut deployment time from 2 hours to 15 minutes and improved reliability.
Mentored 3 junior engineers; established code review guidelines and on-call runbooks adopted across the team.
body:
List skills in a dedicated section and reinforce them in your experience bullets. Use the same terms as the job description where they genuinely apply—ATS and recruiters look for keyword match.

Prefer impact-focused bullets (what you built, for whom, and what improved) over task lists. Tailoring your resume to each role will surface the right mix of languages, systems, and soft skills. WadeCV can help you align your existing experience with software engineer job descriptions and highlight your strongest technical and collaborative wins.

faq.question: How many skills should I list on a software engineer resume?
faq.answer: Focus on 10–15 relevant technical skills plus a few soft skills. Match the job description and reinforce them in your experience bullets rather than listing every technology you've touched.

commonMistakes: Listing outdated or irrelevant technologies
Generic soft skills without evidence in bullets
Keyword stuffing; keep language natural

title: Customer Service Skills for Your Resume or CV (40+ Examples for 2026)
metaDescription: The complete list of customer service skills for your CV or resume in 2026: communication, CRM tools, ticketing platforms, soft skills, and bullet examples for entry, senior, and lead roles.
intro: Recruiters scan customer service CVs for two things: the platforms you've used (Zendesk, Salesforce, Intercom, Gorgias, Freshdesk) and the metrics you've moved (CSAT, NPS, FCR, AHT, ticket volume). This guide gives you 40+ skills grouped into 6 clusters, 12 bullet examples spanning agent through team-lead roles, and the keywords that get past ATS filters in 2026. Whether you call it a resume or a CV, the structure is the same — use the regional spelling that matches the job posting.

skillClusters.name: Communication & customer interaction
items: De-escalation of frustrated or angry customers
Clear written communication for email, chat, and ticket replies
Tone matching (formal, casual, technical) for different audiences
Multilingual support (specify languages and CEFR levels)
Voice clarity and pacing for phone-based roles
Cross-cultural communication for global support teams

skillClusters.name: Helpdesk & CRM platforms
items: Salesforce Service Cloud and Lightning console
Genesys Cloud or NICE for contact-centre operations

skillClusters.name: Channels & ticketing
items: Omnichannel ticket triage (email, chat, voice, social, SMS)
Knowledge base authoring and macro/template creation
Social media response (Twitter/X, Meta, TikTok DMs)
Co-browsing and remote screen-share tools (Surfly, ConnectWise)

skillClusters.name: Problem-solving & process
items: Root-cause analysis using 5 Whys or fishbone diagrams
Process documentation in Notion, Confluence, or Guru
Bug reproduction and triage handoff to engineering

skillClusters.name: Metrics & reporting
items: First Contact Resolution (FCR) and Average Handle Time (AHT)
Reporting in Looker, Tableau, or native CRM dashboards
Forecasting staffing needs against ticket volume

skillClusters.name: Leadership & soft skills
items: Onboarding new hires and writing playbooks
Adaptability across shift work, weekends, on-call
Patience under repetitive or high-volume contact
Conflict resolution between customer and policy
Cross-functional collaboration with Sales, Product, and Engineering

bulletExamples: Resolved 60+ daily customer contacts across email, chat, and phone in Zendesk with 96% CSAT and 87% First Contact Resolution — ranked #1 of 18 agents on quality scorecards three quarters running.
Cut Average Handle Time from 11:24 to 8:02 (–30%) by rebuilding the macro library, deflecting top-10 repeat issues into Help Center articles, and training the team on the new playbook.
Onboarded and coached 7 new hires through 12-week ramp; cohort hit 90% of senior-agent productivity by week 8, two weeks ahead of historical baseline.
Owned tier-2 escalations for billing and account issues; reduced escalation volume to engineering by 42% by documenting common bugs and writing diagnostic decision trees.
Designed and rolled out proactive chat campaign in Intercom; recovered 18% of cart-abandonment sessions and added $240K incremental ARR over six months.
Migrated 2,400 active tickets from legacy Freshdesk instance to Zendesk in 6 weeks with zero data loss; rewrote 180 macros and trained 24 agents on the new workflow.
Authored 64-article knowledge base in Notion; deflected 38% of historical email volume and lifted self-service resolution rate from 21% to 47%.
Led voice-of-customer programme: tagged and analysed 2,000 tickets monthly, surfaced top three friction points to Product, and shipped fixes that reduced repeat contacts 26%.
Promoted to Team Lead after 14 months; managed 9-agent pod, ran weekly QA calibration sessions, and owned the team's CSAT, AHT, and adherence KPIs.
Built shift-coverage forecasting model in Google Sheets that aligned headcount to predicted ticket volume by hour; cut after-hours backlog 55% and reduced overtime spend $38K annually.
Handled bilingual (English/Spanish) support for LATAM expansion; resolved 28% of regional ticket volume single-handedly during the first six months of market entry.
Reduced agent attrition from 34% to 19% YoY by introducing structured 1:1 coaching, clear progression criteria, and a mentorship pairing programme.
body:
Customer service hiring in 2026 is squarely focused on three things: tool fluency, measurable impact, and the ability to scale yourself through documentation and coaching. Generic 'good communication and teamwork' bullets will not move you off page 7 of a recruiter search — specific platforms, specific metrics, and specific scope will.

Lead every bullet with a verb and a number. Recruiters scanning a resume or CV in six seconds want to see ticket volume, CSAT, AHT, FCR, or revenue impact before they read the rest of the line. Even entry-level candidates can quantify: 'handled 40 contacts per day' is more credible than 'handled customer issues'.

For the skills section, mirror the language of the job posting. If the posting says 'Zendesk', do not write 'helpdesk software' — write 'Zendesk Support and Guide'. If it says 'CSAT', do not write 'customer satisfaction'. ATS keyword matching is literal, and recruiters often filter on exact tool names. Including the version or module (Salesforce Service Cloud, not just Salesforce) demonstrates depth.

Progression is the most under-used signal on customer service CVs. Show how you grew: started as agent, moved to senior agent owning escalations, then team lead managing a pod. Hiring managers for senior or lead roles want to see that arc explicitly, not infer it from job titles alone. Use bullets to call out training, coaching, and process improvements that took load off your manager.

Technical depth matters more than ever. Customer service teams in 2026 own AI tooling (Resolution Bot, Einstein Bots, Fin), workflow automation, and increasingly Tableau or Looker dashboards. Listing one or two of these moves you out of the generic-agent pile.

For career changers entering customer service — from retail, hospitality, or admin roles — translate transferable skills using customer-service vocabulary. 'Managed front-of-house at a restaurant' becomes 'resolved 100+ daily guest interactions in person and over the phone, including escalations and billing disputes'. The work is the same; the language is what gets you past the screen.

WadeCV reads each customer service job posting, identifies the exact tools and metrics it asks for, and rewrites your bullets to match — in the regional spelling and terminology the employer uses. Upload your resume or CV, paste the job link, and you'll see a fit analysis in 30 seconds.

faq.question: What are the top customer service skills to put on a resume or CV in 2026?
faq.answer: The skills that get the most weight in 2026 are: helpdesk platform fluency (Zendesk, Salesforce Service Cloud, Intercom), CSAT and FCR metrics, de-escalation, knowledge-base authoring, and voice-of-customer process design. AI tool experience (Resolution Bot, Einstein, Fin) is increasingly mentioned in mid-to-senior job descriptions. List specific tool names rather than generic categories — ATS filters match on exact platform names.

faq.question: How many customer service skills should I list on my CV?
faq.answer: Aim for 8–12 skills in a dedicated skills section, weighted toward what the job description names explicitly. Group them into 2–3 categories (technical tools, customer-handling skills, soft skills) so a recruiter can scan in seconds. Avoid filler like 'hard worker' or 'team player' — every line should either match a posting keyword or be backed up by a bullet in your experience section.

faq.question: What are good customer service bullet points for a resume?
faq.answer: The strongest bullets follow the format 'Action verb + scope + tool + metric'. For example: 'Resolved 50+ daily Zendesk tickets with 95% CSAT' beats 'Handled customer queries'. Always lead with a number where possible — ticket volume, CSAT, NPS, AHT, FCR, headcount trained, or revenue retained. Even at entry level, 'handled 40 contacts per day' is more credible than 'helped customers'.

faq.question: What customer service keywords help my CV pass ATS screening?
faq.answer: Pull keywords directly from the job posting: tool names (Zendesk, Salesforce, Intercom, Freshdesk, HubSpot, Gorgias), metrics (CSAT, NPS, FCR, AHT, CES), channel words (omnichannel, live chat, ticket triage), and methodology terms (SLA, escalation, knowledge base, voice of customer). ATS systems like Greenhouse, Lever, and Workday match on exact strings, so use the same spelling and capitalisation as the posting.

faq.question: How do I write a customer service summary for my resume?
faq.answer: A strong professional summary is 2–3 sentences and includes: years of experience, primary channel/industry (e.g. SaaS phone support, e-commerce live chat), platform fluency (Zendesk, Salesforce), and one headline metric ('maintained 96% CSAT across 12,000 annual tickets'). Tailor this to the role — a B2B SaaS posting wants different language than a high-volume DTC e-commerce role.

faq.question: What customer service skills do I list as a Customer Service Representative vs. a Customer Service Manager?
faq.answer: Reps lead with platform fluency, contact volume, individual CSAT/AHT, and product knowledge. Managers and team leads add: headcount managed, hiring and onboarding, QA calibration, scheduling/forecasting, attrition rate, budget responsibility, and cross-functional partnerships with Product and Engineering. Reps prove they can do the job; managers prove they can scale a team to do it.

faq.question: How do I list customer service skills on my CV if I'm transitioning from retail or hospitality?
faq.answer: Translate every interaction-heavy responsibility into customer-service language. 'Front-of-house service' becomes 'resolved 100+ daily guest interactions including billing escalations'. 'Cashier' becomes 'processed transactions and handled returns/disputes for high-volume retail environment'. List any POS, scheduling, or CRM tools you used — Square, Toast, Lightspeed all count as ticket/transaction systems and translate cleanly to a helpdesk context.

faq.question: Should I say 'customer service' or 'customer experience' on my CV?
faq.answer: Match the job posting. 'Customer service' is more common in operational roles (agent, rep, support specialist) and high-volume contact-centre work. 'Customer experience' (CX) appears more in strategic, design-focused, or SaaS roles where the work spans onboarding, lifecycle, and product feedback. If you've done both, list specific responsibilities under each — 'CX programme design' is meaningfully different from 'CS ticket handling'.

commonMistakes: Listing 'good communication' or 'team player' without backing them up with bullets — these are filler and waste recruiter attention
Writing 'helpdesk software' instead of naming Zendesk, Salesforce, or whichever platform you actually used — ATS filters on exact tool names
Omitting metrics entirely. Even 'handled 30 contacts per day' is better than 'handled customer queries'
Using identical bullets across roles instead of showing progression from agent to senior to lead
Mismatching regional spelling — 'organised' on a US resume or 'organized' on a UK CV signals you didn't read the posting carefully
Burying tool names in paragraphs instead of putting them in a scannable Skills section
Listing irrelevant soft skills (punctual, hardworking) instead of role-specific ones (de-escalation, root-cause analysis, voice of customer)

title: Executive Assistant Skills for Your CV or Resume (40+ Examples for 2026)
metaDescription: Executive assistant CV skills: C-suite support, calendar and travel management, board governance, and tech stack. 40+ ATS keywords, 12 quantified bullet examples, and 8 FAQs for EA roles from coordinator to Chief of Staff.
intro: Executive assistants operate at the highest level of an organisation — managing the schedules, communications, projects, and confidential matters of C-suite and senior leaders. This guide covers the skills, tools, and bullet examples that appear most in EA and senior PA job descriptions, from start-up to FTSE 100.

skillClusters.name: Technology & productivity tools
items: Google Workspace (Gmail, Calendar, Drive, Docs, Slides)

skillClusters.name: Executive office operations
items: Preparation of board reports and board packs

bulletExamples: Supported CEO and CFO at a Series B fintech (140 employees); managed complex multi-timezone calendars, reducing scheduling conflicts by 70% through systematic priority-blocking.
Planned and executed 26 international trips per year for 3 C-suite executives — flights, hotels, ground transport, and briefing documents — achieving zero missed connections in 2 years.
Prepared and distributed board packs for 6 board meetings per year; reduced pack preparation time from 3 days to 1.5 days by building a SharePoint template system.
Managed a £180K executive office budget including travel, events, and supplies; finished 3 consecutive financial years between 2% under and on budget.
Drafted executive communications on behalf of CEO for all-hands meetings, investor updates, and media responses — consistently approved with minimal revisions.
Implemented Concur for expense management across a 12-person senior leadership team, cutting average expense processing time from 14 days to 4 days.
Co-ordinated annual leadership off-site for 35 senior managers — venue, agenda, catering, materials, and AV — delivered on a £45K budget under a 6-week timeline.
Acted as gatekeeper for CEO inbox (averaging 120 emails/day); triaged, drafted responses, and escalated time-sensitive items — enabling CEO to reduce inbox time from 2 hours to 30 minutes per day.
Supported company acquisition due diligence over 6 weeks, co-ordinating document requests and NDA management between legal, finance, and 4 external advisors.
Built and maintained an executive contact CRM in Salesforce for the CEO's network of 340 strategic contacts, including relationship notes, last-contact dates, and next-action items.
Trained 3 junior EAs and PAs on calendar management systems, board pack preparation, and travel booking processes — reducing EA team onboarding time from 6 weeks to 3.
Served as de facto Chief of Staff for 4 months during transition period — ran weekly leadership team meetings, tracked 12 strategic initiatives, and managed 3 cross-departmental projects.
body:
The strongest executive assistant CVs demonstrate two things: the seniority of the executives supported (CEO, CFO, Board-level, C-suite) and the measurable impact of the support (time saved, events delivered, systems built, processes improved).

Scope signals matter: state the number of executives you supported, the size of the organisation, whether you worked in a listed company, a PE-backed business, or a start-up, and whether you had budget responsibility. An EA supporting a CEO at a 10-person start-up is a different profile from one supporting a Board at a FTSE 100 firm — both are valid, but they need to be explicit.

Technology fluency is increasingly important for EA roles in 2026. Hiring managers expect proficiency in Microsoft 365 (especially Outlook and SharePoint) or Google Workspace, plus at least one expense management tool (Concur, Expensify), one project tracking tool (Asana, Monday.com, Notion), and ideally familiarity with AI tools (Copilot, ChatGPT) for drafting support.

Boarding governance experience — board pack preparation, committee secretariat, minute-taking at board level — is a strong differentiator for EA roles at director level and above. If you have it, give it its own section or cluster.

WadeCV can tailor your EA profile to the specific executive level, sector, and technology stack each employer is hiring for.

faq.question: What skills should an executive assistant put on their CV?
faq.answer: The core skills hiring managers look for in EA CVs are: complex calendar and diary management, executive travel planning, board and governance support, written communication and drafting, stakeholder management and discretion, expense management, and proficiency with productivity tools (Microsoft 365 or Google Workspace, Concur, project tracking tools). For senior EA or PA roles, also include: board pack preparation, minute-taking, project co-ordination, and budget management.

faq.question: How do I write a strong executive assistant bullet point?
faq.answer: Lead with the seniority of the executive(s) supported, then state the scope, and close with the outcome or improvement. Example: 'Managed complex multi-timezone diary for CEO and CFO (Series B, 140 employees); reduced scheduling conflicts by 70% through systematic priority-blocking and advance-booking protocols.' The format is: who you supported + what you did + measurable result. Avoid vague bullets like 'provided administrative support to senior leadership' — they do not differentiate.

faq.question: What is the difference between an executive assistant and a personal assistant?
faq.answer: In practice the titles overlap, but EA roles typically involve more strategic support: project co-ordination, board governance, stakeholder management, and business operations. PA roles tend to focus more on personal organisation: travel, diary, personal expenses, and household management in some cases. For FTSE and large corporate roles, EA implies C-suite level support and often includes budget responsibility. For start-ups, the same person may hold both PA and EA responsibilities under one title.

faq.question: How do I show executive assistant experience if I supported a team, not an individual executive?
faq.answer: Frame it around the seniority of the team and the scope of the support. 'Supported a 7-person senior leadership team (VP level and above) with diary management, travel planning, and board pack preparation' is stronger than 'provided admin support to the senior team.' Quantify wherever possible: number of people supported, volume of travel bookings, frequency of board meetings, budget managed.

faq.question: What tools and software should I know as an executive assistant in 2026?
faq.answer: Core tools for EA roles in 2026: Microsoft Outlook (complex shared calendar management), Teams and SharePoint (document management and executive channels), PowerPoint and Word (board pack and executive presentation preparation), Concur or Expensify (expense management), Zoom or Teams (executive meeting management), and DocuSign (contract routing). Increasingly valued: Asana or Monday.com for project tracking, Salesforce for executive contact management, and AI tools like Copilot or ChatGPT for drafting support.

faq.question: Should I include board governance experience on my EA CV?
faq.answer: Absolutely — board support experience (board pack preparation, committee secretariat, AGM logistics, minute-taking at board level) is a significant differentiator for senior EA, Chief of Staff, and PA to the Board roles. If you have it, give it a dedicated bullets cluster rather than burying it in a general 'administration' bullet. Companies House filings, NED liaison, and governance documentation experience are particularly valued in listed companies and regulated industries.

faq.question: How long should an executive assistant CV be?
faq.answer: Two pages is standard for EA CVs at most career stages. The EA role requires demonstrating breadth of support — one page risks omitting key skills. Three pages is acceptable for very senior EAs (10+ years, board-level support, Chief of Staff experience), but only if every bullet shows genuine scope or outcome. Prioritise quality over length: 8 strong quantified bullets outperform 16 vague ones.

faq.question: What keywords improve an executive assistant CV for ATS screening?
faq.answer: High-frequency ATS keywords for EA roles include: diary management, calendar management, travel management, board support, stakeholder management, minute-taking, board packs, expense management, Concur, Microsoft Office 365, PowerPoint, SharePoint, confidentiality, discretion, C-suite support, CEO support, governance, project co-ordination, and event management. Mirror the exact terms from the job description — most EA JDs specify the exact tools and executive levels required.

commonMistakes: Not stating the seniority of executives supported — 'senior leadership' is vague; say CEO, CFO, Board, VP-level
No metrics at all — time saved, meetings managed per month, travel trips per year, budget size, events delivered are all quantifiable
Listing 'Microsoft Office' without specifying which tools and at what level — ATS systems scan for 'Outlook', 'PowerPoint', 'SharePoint' individually
Burying board governance experience in a general admin bullet — if you prepared board packs or took board minutes, that deserves its own bullet
No mention of company context — 'FTSE 100', 'Series B tech company', 'PE-backed business', '500-person organisation' sets the calibre of the role immediately
Using the word 'support' for every bullet — vary with co-ordinated, managed, prepared, facilitated, implemented, drafted, delivered
Omitting technology tools entirely — every EA CV in 2026 should list the specific tools used (Concur, Asana, DocuSign, etc.)
A generic personal statement — the opening summary should name the level of executive supported and one specific achievement, not 'highly organised professional with excellent communication skills'

title: Accountant Skills for Your Resume or CV (40+ Examples & ATS Keywords for 2026)
metaDescription: Complete accountant skills for your CV or resume in 2026: GAAP/IFRS, ERP systems, reporting, audit, and bullet examples for staff, senior, and manager-level roles. ATS keywords included.
intro: Accounting CVs in 2026 win on three signals: technical depth (GAAP, IFRS, tax code), system fluency (NetSuite, SAP, Workday, QuickBooks, Sage), and ownership scope (entities closed, audits supported, headcount managed). Generic 'detail-oriented and deadline-driven' bullets do not move the needle — specific systems, specific frameworks, and specific outcomes do. This guide gives you 40+ accounting skills grouped into 6 clusters, 12 quantified bullet examples spanning staff to manager-level roles, and the exact ATS keywords recruiters and Big Four firms search for in 2026.

skillClusters.name: Technical accounting & frameworks
items: IFRS (especially IFRS 15, 16, 9 for revenue, leases, financial instruments)
Indirect tax (VAT, GST, sales tax) and direct tax basics

items: Oracle Fusion Cloud and Oracle EBS

items: Financial statement preparation (P&L, balance sheet, cash flow)
Excel (pivot tables, XLOOKUP, Power Query, modelling)
Power BI, Tableau, Looker for finance dashboards
SQL for finance analytics (BigQuery, Snowflake basics)

skillClusters.name: Audit, controls & compliance
items: External audit liaison (Big Four and mid-tier)
Internal controls and segregation of duties
PCAOB and IAASB audit standards exposure
Fraud detection and forensic accounting basics

items: Federal, state, and local corporate tax preparation
VAT/GST returns and indirect tax filings
Cash flow forecasting and treasury operations

skillClusters.name: Leadership & soft skills
items: Cross-functional collaboration with FP&A, Treasury, Legal
Mentoring junior accountants and CPA candidates
Project management for system implementations and migrations
Change management for process and policy changes

bulletExamples: Owned month-end close for 4 US entities and 2 UK subsidiaries on NetSuite OneWorld; reduced close cycle from 9 to 5 business days through reconciliation automation in BlackLine and a redesigned close checklist.

Led ASC 606 revenue recognition implementation for $42M SaaS subscription business; documented policy memo, built revenue waterfall in NetSuite ARM, and trained 6-person finance team. Audit completed with zero material adjustments.
Prepared and reviewed monthly financial statements for $180M division; explained variances to CFO and division GM, owning all P&L, balance sheet, and cash flow presentation.

Managed Big Four annual audit (PwC) end-to-end: built PBC tracker, owned 280 audit requests, and resolved technical positions on revenue and lease accounting. Audit closed two weeks ahead of schedule.
Implemented SOX 404 control framework for newly-public SaaS company; documented 64 key controls, designed quarterly testing programme, and remediated 3 deficiencies before year-end attestation.
Migrated general ledger from QuickBooks to NetSuite for $28M e-commerce business in 14 weeks; configured chart of accounts, multi-book setup, and integrated Shopify, Stripe, and ADP payroll.
Reconciled 75+ balance sheet accounts monthly; identified and corrected $340K cumulative misstatement in inventory reserve and implemented new monthly review controls to prevent recurrence.
Prepared US federal and 14-state corporate income tax returns for $95M consolidated group; worked with EY on R&D tax credit study generating $620K in credits.
Built three-statement consolidation model in Excel for 9 entities across US, UK, and Singapore; replaced 14 disconnected workbooks and cut consolidation time from 3 days to 4 hours.
Hired, onboarded, and managed 4 staff accountants; promoted 2 to senior level within 18 months and held 100% retention across the team.
Led implementation of FloQast close automation across 12-person accounting team; cut reconciliation review time 38% and improved close-to-report cycle by 2 days.
Prepared monthly VAT returns and EC Sales Lists for UK and EU operations; managed HMRC enquiry and resolved £85K refund within statutory deadline.
body:
An accounting CV in 2026 is read first by an ATS that matches keywords against the job description, and second by a hiring manager scanning for system fluency, technical depth, and scope of ownership. Vague bullets like 'prepared financial statements' or 'helped with audit' lose immediately to candidates who write 'prepared monthly financial statements for $180M division' or 'managed Big Four audit (PwC) end-to-end with 280 PBC requests'.

Lead every bullet with scope and an outcome. Scope is entities (number and revenue), accounts (count or category), audits (firm and timeline), or budgets (size). Outcomes are close-cycle days, audit adjustments avoided, errors corrected, or hours saved through automation. 'Reconciled 75+ accounts monthly with zero material adjustments at year-end' is a stronger line than 'performed reconciliations'.

ERP system fluency is the single highest-leverage signal on an accounting CV. Recruiters and Big Four firms filter directly on system names — NetSuite, SAP S/4HANA, Workday, Oracle Fusion, QuickBooks, Sage Intacct, Xero. List the specific module if you've used one ('NetSuite OneWorld with multi-book' beats 'NetSuite'). Cloud ERP experience (NetSuite, Workday, Oracle Fusion, Dynamics 365) is in higher demand than legacy on-prem systems and pays meaningfully more in 2026.

Close-automation tooling has become a near-table-stakes skill at mid-market and above. BlackLine, FloQast, Trintech, and Adra are explicitly listed in many controller and accounting manager job postings. If you've used one, put it on your CV with the outcome (cycle time reduced, controls automated, FTE hours saved). If you haven't, basic familiarity through a free trial or a Coursera/Udemy course can be enough to claim awareness.

For public accounting candidates moving to industry, translate audit experience into industry-relevant terms. 'Audit senior on $500M manufacturing client' becomes 'Owned year-end audit fieldwork for $500M client; led 4-person team across revenue, inventory, and PP&E; identified $280K control deficiency and presented remediation plan to client CFO'. The work is the same; the framing has to match the role you want.

For career changers entering accounting from bookkeeping, finance, or analyst roles, certifications carry weight. Listing 'CPA candidate (3 of 4 sections passed)', 'ACCA Part-Qualified', or 'CIMA passed finalist' immediately signals direction and commitment. Pair with QuickBooks ProAdvisor, Xero Advisor, or NetSuite Foundation certifications for stronger SMB roles.

If you write a CV (UK, EU, India, Australia) rather than a resume (US, Canada), match the regional spelling and framework focus. UK CVs typically use IFRS, ACCA/ACA/ICAEW certifications, and £ figures; US resumes use GAAP, CPA, and $ figures. Multinational candidates should call out dual fluency — 'IFRS and US GAAP technical reviews' is a high-value signal.

WadeCV reads each accounting job description and rewrites your CV to mirror the systems, frameworks, and reporting cadence it specifies — in the right regional spelling and accounting standard. Upload your CV, paste the job link, and you'll see a fit analysis with the gaps highlighted in 30 seconds.

faq.question: What are the most important accountant skills to put on a CV or resume in 2026?
faq.answer: Technical: GAAP or IFRS depending on region, ASC 606 revenue recognition, ASC 842 leases, consolidations, and SOX 404 controls. Systems: ERP fluency (NetSuite, SAP S/4HANA, Workday, Oracle Fusion, QuickBooks, Sage Intacct, Xero) is the single highest-leverage signal. Process: month-end close cycle, reconciliations, audit liaison. Tooling: Excel (advanced), BlackLine or FloQast for close automation, and increasingly basic SQL or Power BI for finance analytics.

faq.question: What accounting keywords should I include for ATS to pick up my resume?
faq.answer: Pull keywords directly from the posting: framework names (GAAP, IFRS, ASC 606, ASC 842, SOX 404), ERP names with exact spelling (NetSuite, SAP S/4HANA, Workday, Oracle Fusion, QuickBooks, Sage Intacct, Xero), close-automation tools (BlackLine, FloQast, Trintech), certifications (CPA, ACCA, ACA, CIMA, CMA, CA), and process terms (month-end close, reconciliation, audit, consolidation, intercompany). ATS systems like Greenhouse, Workday Recruiting, and iCIMS match exact strings, so use the same capitalisation as the posting.

faq.question: What are good accountant resume bullet examples?
faq.answer: Lead with scope and an outcome. Strong examples: 'Owned month-end close for 4 US entities on NetSuite OneWorld; reduced close cycle from 9 to 5 business days', 'Managed Big Four audit (PwC) end-to-end with 280 PBC requests; closed two weeks ahead of schedule', 'Reconciled 75+ balance sheet accounts monthly; identified and corrected $340K cumulative misstatement in inventory reserve'. Avoid generic bullets like 'prepared financial statements' or 'helped with audit' — they could apply to any accountant at any level.

faq.question: Should I list QuickBooks, NetSuite, and SAP all on my CV if I've used them?
faq.answer: Yes — ERP fluency is the highest-leverage signal on an accounting CV and recruiters filter on exact system names. List each system with the depth signal: module used (NetSuite OneWorld, SAP FI/CO), version (S/4HANA vs ECC), and the most senior task you owned in it (configured chart of accounts, owned month-end close, designed integrations). Three deep ERP listings beats six shallow ones.

faq.question: How do I write an accountant CV with no experience or as a graduate?
faq.answer: Lead with certifications in progress (CPA candidate, ACCA Part-Qualified, CIMA finalist), relevant coursework (intermediate accounting, cost accounting, audit), and any internship or part-time bookkeeping work. List specific systems even from coursework or self-study (QuickBooks, Xero, Excel modelling). Quantify any internship work — 'reconciled 30 accounts monthly during 12-week internship' is more credible than 'gained accounting experience'. Add free certifications: QuickBooks ProAdvisor, Xero Advisor, NetSuite Foundation, or Coursera financial accounting courses signal initiative.

faq.question: Should I list CPA or ACCA on my accountant resume?
faq.answer: Yes — always. Active CPA, ACA, ACCA, CIMA, CMA, or CA designations belong directly under your name in the header (e.g. 'Jane Smith, CPA') and in your professional summary. Candidate status counts too: 'CPA candidate, 3 of 4 sections passed' or 'ACCA Part-Qualified (12 of 14 papers)'. State licence (US) or country/jurisdiction (international) helps for cross-border applications. Lapsed credentials should be left off unless reactivation is genuinely planned.

faq.question: What's the difference between an accountant CV in the UK vs US?
faq.answer: UK accountant CVs typically reference IFRS, ACCA/ACA/ICAEW or CIMA designations, HMRC and VAT compliance, and £ figures. US accountant resumes reference US GAAP, CPA designation by state, IRS/state tax compliance, and $ figures. UK CVs run 2 pages comfortably; US resumes target 1–2 pages. Multinational candidates should highlight dual standards fluency ('IFRS and US GAAP technical reviews' or 'managed UK and US year-end close') as a key differentiator for global roles.

faq.question: How many accounting skills should I list on my CV?
faq.answer: 10–18 specific skills grouped into 3–4 categories (Technical Accounting, ERP & Systems, Close & Reporting, Audit & Compliance). Lead with what the posting names. Avoid filler like 'attention to detail' or 'team player' — every line should either match a posting keyword (GAAP, NetSuite, SOX) or be backed up by a quantified bullet in your experience section. For specialist roles (Tax Manager, Audit Senior, Revenue Accountant), go deeper in one area rather than wider across all functions.

commonMistakes: Writing 'prepared financial statements' without entity count, scope, or audience — every accountant claims this
Listing 'ERP' or 'accounting software' instead of NetSuite, SAP S/4HANA, QuickBooks by name — ATS filters on exact tool names
Omitting close-cycle metrics (days to close, reconciliation count, audit adjustments) which are the most quantifiable accounting outcomes
Forgetting close-automation tooling (BlackLine, FloQast) — these are explicitly named in mid-market and above job postings
Listing 'CPA' without state, exam progress, or active status — recruiters need to know if you're licensed
Mixing GAAP and IFRS terminology without context — specify which framework applied to which role
Burying ERP system names in paragraph form instead of putting them in a scannable Skills section
Generic 'audit experience' instead of naming the firm (PwC, EY, KPMG, Deloitte, BDO, Grant Thornton, RSM) and the audit type

bulletExamples: Led Lean transformation across 3 production lines, reducing average cycle time by 22% and saving £380K annually in labour and rework costs.
Owned £4.2M operations budget across 2 sites; ended each of 3 consecutive financial years 4-7% under budget without cutting headcount.
Built and scaled a 45-person operations team over 18 months, reducing voluntary attrition from 28% to 14% through structured onboarding and weekly 1:1s.
Deployed NetSuite WMS across a 3-site logistics network, cutting pick-and-pack error rate from 3.1% to 0.4% and improving same-day dispatch from 78% to 96%.
Achieved ISO 9001:2015 recertification for a 200-person manufacturing site with zero major non-conformances — first clean audit in 4 years.
Renegotiated 7 supplier contracts, consolidating 3PL providers from 4 to 2, saving £210K annually while improving on-time delivery from 87% to 96%.
Implemented weekly Kaizen events across packing operations; generated 38 employee-led improvements over 6 months, reducing waste by 31%.
Managed site P&L of £12M, identifying £640K in cost reduction opportunities through headcount restructuring and energy-efficiency projects.

body:
Operations manager resumes are judged on measurable improvement. Every major bullet should show a before/after: cost reduced, time saved, quality improved, output scaled, team built. Reviewers want to see the scope (budget size, team headcount, number of sites) alongside the outcome.

Lean or Six Sigma certification (Green Belt, Black Belt) differentiates candidates, especially in manufacturing, logistics, and healthcare operations. Even if you are self-taught in Lean principles, list the methodology and give a specific project example.

Industry-specific tools matter: manufacturing ops managers should list SAP or Oracle; retail and e-commerce ops managers should include WMS and TMS platforms; tech and SaaS ops managers should mention Jira, Notion, and data tools like Power BI. Generic 'ERP systems' or 'project management tools' are keyword-weak — name the specific platform.

For roles with a financial scope, state the budget or P&L owned explicitly. A bullet saying 'managed operations budget' is weaker than 'owned £3.8M site budget'. UK hiring managers assess scope quickly by scanning for numbers — make yours visible.

WadeCV can tailor your operations profile to the specific industry, team size, and system stack each employer is hiring for. Paste the job URL and WadeCV identifies which skill clusters and keywords to prioritise.

faq.question: Is Six Sigma certification required for an operations manager job?
faq.answer: Not required, but it is a strong differentiator — especially in manufacturing, logistics, and healthcare operations. Even a Green Belt signals commitment to process methodology. Many employers list it as 'preferred' rather than required. If you have not completed formal certification, list the methodology ('Lean Six Sigma — self-directed, Green Belt candidate') and provide a project example with measurable results.

faq.question: What skills should I put on my operations manager resume?
faq.answer: Prioritise skills that match the specific job description, but the core cluster most ops manager JDs require includes: process improvement methodology (Lean, Six Sigma, Kaizen), financial management (budget ownership, cost reduction, P&L), people leadership (team management, performance reviews, hiring), technology tools (SAP, Oracle, ERP systems, Power BI), and supply chain or logistics knowledge depending on the industry. Always mirror the exact terms from the job description — ATS systems match keywords precisely.

faq.question: How do I show operations management skills without a formal operations title?
faq.answer: Focus on outcomes, not titles. If you managed a team, owned a budget, drove a process improvement, or oversaw logistics — those are operations skills, regardless of your job title. Write bullets that quantify the scope and result: 'Managed fulfilment operations for a 12-person team (£2.1M GMV quarterly), reducing shipping errors by 60%.' The skills section should list the specific tools and methodologies you used, matching the terminology of the ops manager roles you are targeting.

faq.question: What is the difference between an operations manager and a general manager resume?
faq.answer: An operations manager resume emphasises execution: process efficiency, cost management, team performance metrics, and specific system tools. A general manager resume places more weight on P&L ownership, strategic direction, commercial growth, and cross-functional leadership. If you are targeting both titles, your resume should lead with whichever skill set is stronger — but both roles benefit from quantified operational results.

faq.question: Should I include PRINCE2 or PMP on my operations manager resume?
faq.answer: Yes, if you hold the certification — especially in UK operations roles where PRINCE2 is widely recognised. Project management certifications signal structured delivery capability, which matters for ERP implementations, site expansions, and operational transformation projects. If you do not hold a formal PM certification but have led major projects, describe the project size, timeline, and outcome in your bullets instead.

faq.question: How long should an operations manager resume be?
faq.answer: Two pages is standard for most operations manager roles. A one-page resume will likely omit the scope and quantified results that differentiate strong ops candidates. Three pages is acceptable for very senior roles (Head of Operations, VP Operations) with board-level experience, multiple site responsibility, or extensive project history. Keep every bullet outcome-focused — remove generic responsibilities to maintain two clean pages.

faq.question: What operations manager keywords improve ATS ranking?
faq.answer: The highest-frequency ATS keywords in operations manager job descriptions include: Lean manufacturing, Six Sigma, Kaizen, process improvement, P&L, budget management, SAP, Oracle, ERP, cost reduction, continuous improvement, KPI management, supply chain, inventory management, vendor management, workforce management, SOP, ISO 9001, stakeholder management, and operational efficiency. Mirror the exact terms from the job description — ATS systems are pattern-matching engines and reward exact keyword matches.

faq.question: How do I write a professional summary for an operations manager resume?
faq.answer: Lead with your level (senior/mid-level), industry context, and your single strongest measurable outcome. Then add your two or three most relevant specialisms for the specific role. Example: 'Operations Manager with 10 years in FMCG manufacturing. Led Lean transformation across 4 production lines, achieving 25% cycle time reduction and £1.2M annual cost saving. Experienced in SAP S/4HANA, ISO 9001 compliance, and cross-site team leadership (up to 80 FTE).' Keep it to 3 sentences — hiring managers spend 6-8 seconds on the summary.

commonMistakes: Describing scope without outcomes — 'managed daily operations across 3 sites' needs a 'resulting in X' or 'achieving Y%' to be useful
No financial metrics — every ops manager CV should state the budget owned, cost saved, or revenue protected
Omitting Lean / Six Sigma training even if informal — list the methodology and give one specific project with numbers
Generic tool names — 'ERP systems' and 'project management tools' are weaker than 'SAP S/4HANA' and 'Smartsheet'
Mixing responsibilities and achievements in the same bullet — split them or combine into a single outcome-focused bullet
No team size — always state the headcount managed, even approximately ('team of ~30', '45 direct and indirect reports')
Underselling multi-site experience — if you managed multiple sites or shifts, make that visible in the intro bullet, not buried in the description
Listing IOSH or health and safety qualifications in the education section instead of the skills or certifications section where ATS systems scan for them

Designed and launched referral programme in Friendbuy; 11% of new customer acquisition now comes from referrals at 70% lower CAC than paid social.
body:
A digital marketing CV in 2026 lives or dies on three signals: channels owned, platforms named, and metrics moved. Recruiters and hiring managers scan in seconds for the specific tools and the specific outcomes — not for adjectives like 'creative' or 'data-driven'.

The single biggest mistake is writing 'managed social media' or 'ran ads'. Everyone's done that. Write 'scaled Meta Ads from $80K to $640K monthly at 3.8x ROAS' or 'grew TikTok from 0 to 84K followers in 9 months'. The numbers are what hiring managers screen for, and they are what get your CV into the shortlist pile.

Tool fluency is now table stakes. List the platforms by name (Klaviyo, not 'email tool'; Ahrefs, not 'SEO software'; GA4, not 'analytics'). ATS systems like Greenhouse, Lever, and Workday match on exact strings, and recruiter Boolean searches do too. The most over-hired-for tools in 2026 digital marketing job postings are: Google Ads, Meta Ads Manager, GA4, HubSpot, Klaviyo, Ahrefs/SEMrush, and increasingly AI tooling like ChatGPT, Claude, Surfer, and Jasper.

AI fluency has moved from differentiator to must-have in the last 12 months. Job descriptions for senior marketers explicitly list 'experience using AI tools in content/campaign workflows' or 'familiarity with ChatGPT, Claude, or equivalent for marketing applications'. If you've used these tools to ship work, put them on your CV with a concrete outcome — 'cut blog production cost from £380 to £110 per piece using ChatGPT + Surfer workflow' is far stronger than 'familiar with AI tools'.

For analytics-heavy roles, technical depth pays. Even basic SQL fluency, server-side tracking experience, or attribution modelling work moves you out of the generalist pile. List the warehouses (BigQuery, Snowflake), the CDPs (Segment, mParticle), and the attribution methodologies (data-driven, MMM, multi-touch) you've actually used.

For leadership roles, lead with budget owned, headcount managed, and revenue or pipeline impact. 'Led 6-person team owning £1.8M annual budget; grew MRR 142% over 18 months' beats five paragraphs of strategy buzzwords. CMOs and VPs hiring you want to see whether you can move the P&L.

If you are writing a CV (UK, EU, Australia, India) rather than a resume (US, Canada), match the regional spelling and terminology of the posting. 'Optimised', 'organisations', and 'analyse' for British English; 'optimized', 'organizations', and 'analyze' for American. Recruiters notice mismatches as a signal you did not read the posting carefully.

WadeCV reads each digital marketing job description and rewrites your CV to mirror the channels, tools, and metrics it specifies — in the right regional spelling. Upload your CV, paste the job link, and you'll see a fit analysis with the gaps highlighted in 30 seconds.

faq.question: What are the most in-demand digital marketing skills for a CV in 2026?
faq.answer: Paid media (Google Ads, Meta Ads, LinkedIn Ads), marketing analytics (GA4, Looker Studio, attribution modelling), SEO with technical depth, lifecycle email (Klaviyo, HubSpot), and AI tool fluency (ChatGPT, Claude, Surfer SEO) are the most-cited skills in 2026 job descriptions. Server-side tracking and SQL for marketing analytics are increasingly differentiators for senior roles.

faq.question: What digital marketing keywords should I put on my resume to pass ATS?
faq.answer: Pull keywords directly from the posting: tool names (Google Ads, Meta Ads Manager, GA4, HubSpot, Klaviyo, Ahrefs, SEMrush), metric abbreviations (ROAS, CAC, LTV, CPA, CTR), channel terms (paid social, programmatic, lifecycle email, organic search), and methodology (attribution, A/B testing, CRO, MMM). ATS filters match exact strings, so use the same capitalisation and spelling as the posting — 'GA4' beats 'Google Analytics 4' if that's how the posting writes it.

faq.question: How do I quantify digital marketing achievements on my CV?
faq.answer: Every bullet should answer 'how much, how many, by what %, vs. what baseline'. Use percentage growth (organic traffic +187% YoY), absolute spend (£420K Google Ads budget), efficiency metrics (CPA reduced 34%), and revenue impact (24% of monthly revenue from email programme). If you don't have access to numbers, use proxies: posts published, campaigns launched, headcount managed, accounts targeted.

faq.question: Should I list AI tools like ChatGPT and Claude on my marketing CV?
faq.answer: Yes — in 2026, AI fluency is explicitly listed in most senior digital marketing job descriptions. List the specific tools (ChatGPT, Claude, Jasper, Surfer, Midjourney, Runway) and pair them with concrete outcomes ('cut blog production cost 71% using ChatGPT + Surfer workflow'). Listing AI tools without an outcome looks like padding; listing them with measurable impact looks like leverage.

faq.question: What's the difference between digital marketing CV and resume in the UK vs US?
faq.answer: Functionally similar, but a UK CV is often slightly longer (2 pages is standard, not unusual to see 3) and uses British spelling ('optimised', 'organisation', 'analyse'). US resumes target 1–2 pages and use American spelling. Salary expectations are rarely included in either. Photo, date of birth, and marital status should be excluded from both — these are still common in some EU CVs but increasingly considered outdated.

faq.question: How do I write a digital marketing CV with no experience or as a career changer?
faq.answer: Lead with measurable side projects, certifications, and freelance work. Run a personal blog and grew it to X visitors? That's an SEO portfolio. Managed Meta Ads for a friend's small business? That's paid media experience with real budget and ROAS. List relevant certifications (Google Ads, Meta Blueprint, HubSpot Inbound, GA4) and any AI tool experience. Career changers should translate prior roles into marketing language — 'data analyst' becomes 'marketing analytics and attribution', 'sales rep' becomes 'B2B pipeline generation and CRM management'.

faq.question: How long should the skills section on a digital marketing CV be?
faq.answer: 8–15 specific skills grouped into 2–4 categories (e.g. Paid Media, SEO & Content, Analytics, Marketing Automation). Avoid filler like 'Microsoft Office' or 'attention to detail'. Every line should either match a posting keyword or be backed up by a quantified bullet in your experience section. For specialist roles (e.g. SEO Lead, Paid Acquisition Manager), go deeper in one area rather than wider across all channels.

faq.question: Should I list social media platforms or just 'social media management' on my CV?
faq.answer: Always list specific platforms — 'LinkedIn, Instagram, TikTok, YouTube' beats 'social media'. Hiring for B2B SaaS wants LinkedIn experience; DTC e-commerce wants Meta and TikTok; B2C entertainment wants TikTok and YouTube. Listing platforms also lets you show channel-specific results (Instagram +84K followers, TikTok +220K, LinkedIn 4M impressions) which are more credible than aggregate 'social media engagement' numbers.

commonMistakes: Writing 'managed social media' or 'ran campaigns' without naming the platform, budget, or outcome — every digital marketer claims this
Listing tool categories ('email tool', 'analytics platform') instead of specific platforms (Klaviyo, GA4) — ATS filters on exact tool names
Omitting metrics on paid campaigns — ROAS, CPA, CAC, and budget owned are non-negotiable for any paid-media role
Forgetting AI tools — in 2026 job descriptions explicitly request ChatGPT, Claude, or equivalent fluency
Using American spelling on a UK posting (or vice versa) — signals you didn't tailor the CV to the role
Burying budget figures and channel mix in paragraphs instead of leading every bullet with a number
Listing every platform you've ever logged into — depth in 4–5 tools beats shallow exposure to 20
No mention of attribution methodology for senior analytics or growth roles — last-click vs data-driven vs MMM is a common interview question

faq.question: Should I list both PyTorch and TensorFlow on my ML resume?

title: Python Developer Skills for Your Resume or CV (50+ Examples for 2026)
metaDescription: Python skills to list on your resume in 2026: 50+ specific frameworks, tools, and keywords across web, data, ML, DevOps, and AI. ATS-optimised bullet examples for junior to senior Python roles.
intro: Python is the most in-demand programming language across web development, data engineering, machine learning, DevOps automation, and AI integrations. Employers want to see specific frameworks and libraries alongside real projects — not just 'Python' as a bullet point. This guide covers the Python skill clusters that appear most frequently in job descriptions, with quantified bullet examples and common resume mistakes to avoid.
bulletExamples: Built a FastAPI microservice processing 12K requests/second with sub-20ms p99 latency; deployed on Kubernetes with HPA auto-scaling and zero-downtime blue/green deployments.
Wrote automated ETL pipeline in Python/Airflow ingesting 50GB/day from 15 sources into Snowflake; reduced analyst data-prep time from 3 hours to 4 minutes.
Refactored legacy Django monolith into async FastAPI services; improved throughput 4× and cut AWS spend by $42K/year through right-sized ECS tasks.

Fine-tuned Llama 3 on internal support tickets using Hugging Face PEFT/LoRA; deployed via FastAPI to handle 800+ support queries/day with 91% resolution accuracy, reducing Tier-1 ticket volume by 40%.
Built LangChain-powered RAG pipeline over 200K internal documents (Pinecone + OpenAI embeddings); reduced average research time for analysts from 45 min to 3 min.
Designed dbt + Airflow data warehouse on BigQuery (8TB, 120+ models); enabled self-serve analytics for 35 stakeholders and eliminated 6 ad-hoc SQL requests/week.
Migrated monolithic PostgreSQL schema (22 tables, 50M rows) to multi-tenant architecture with Alembic migrations; achieved zero-downtime deploy with <2ms query regression.
Led backend team of 5 engineers to ship Python/FastAPI core platform from 0 to production in 11 weeks; service now handles 2M API calls/day with 99.95% uptime.
Wrote pytest suite covering 94% of business logic (620 tests); integrated with GitHub Actions CI, cutting regression escapes from 4/month to 0 in 6 months.
Built real-time fraud detection pipeline with PySpark on Databricks, processing 4M events/hour; reduced false-positive rate from 12% to 3.1%, saving $2.8M/year in manual review costs.
Developed Python CLI tool (Click + Rich) to automate infra provisioning across 3 AWS accounts; reduced onboarding time for new engineers from 2 days to 90 minutes.
Integrated OpenAI Assistants API into Django app; built threaded conversation engine with Celery task queue and Redis caching, serving 8K active users with <500ms median response.
body:
Python developer resumes must communicate three things clearly: your domain specialisation (web, data, ML, DevOps, AI), your framework depth, and your engineering maturity.

Domain first. Python spans too many disciplines for a generic list to be useful. Backend web developers need FastAPI/Django/Flask front-and-centre. Data engineers lead with Airflow, dbt, PySpark, and cloud connectors. ML engineers show PyTorch/TensorFlow plus MLflow/W&B for experiment tracking. DevOps/platform engineers feature Docker, Kubernetes, Terraform CDK, and CI tooling. Structure your skills section to match your primary domain — recruiters scan for category-level signals before reading individual tools.

Framework depth over breadth. Listing 30 libraries without context signals surface-level familiarity. Pair each major framework with an achievement: what you built, at what scale, with what outcome. 'FastAPI (built auth service handling 8K RPM at 12ms p99)' is worth more than a list of 10 framework names.

Engineering maturity signals. Senior engineers demonstrate type safety (mypy, pyright), testing coverage (pytest, factory_boy, Hypothesis), code quality automation (Ruff, Black, pre-commit), and asynchronous programming (asyncio, async/await). Include at least one bullet that references your testing or code quality practices — this differentiates mid-level from senior candidates.

AI and LLM fluency is now a differentiator. In 2026, Python developers who can integrate LLMs — LangChain, LlamaIndex, Hugging Face, OpenAI/Anthropic APIs — command a meaningful salary premium. If you have shipped anything with an LLM API, RAG pipeline, or fine-tuned model, it belongs prominently on your resume.

UK note: Python developer roles in the UK often specify 'Python developer' or 'Python engineer' rather than 'software engineer'. Mirror the exact job-title language in your summary. Mentioning GDPR-compliant data handling or SOC 2 experience is also valued in UK/EU financial and healthtech roles.

WadeCV can tailor your Python skills section to match the specific domain, framework stack, and seniority level of each role you apply for — including rewriting your bullets to mirror the exact language in the job description.

faq.question: Should I list all Python frameworks I know on my resume?
faq.answer: List the frameworks most relevant to the role you are applying for. A backend web role wants FastAPI/Django/Flask; a data engineering role wants Airflow/dbt/PySpark/Pandas; an ML role wants PyTorch/scikit-learn/Hugging Face. Including frameworks you used only briefly — or only know from tutorials — wastes space and can backfire in technical interviews. Tailor your skills section to each application.

faq.question: What Python skills are most in demand in 2026?
faq.answer: Based on current job postings: FastAPI and async Python for backend web; Airflow, dbt, and Polars for data engineering; PyTorch and Hugging Face Transformers for ML; LangChain, LlamaIndex, and OpenAI/Anthropic API integration for AI-adjacent roles; Kubernetes and AWS Lambda for platform/DevOps. Type safety (mypy/pyright) and testing (pytest) are increasingly expected at senior level across all domains.

faq.question: How should a junior Python developer write their resume?
faq.answer: Focus on projects over professional experience if your work history is limited. For each project, name the specific frameworks used, what problem it solved, and any measurable outcome (e.g., response time, data volume processed, users served). Include your GitHub profile. Show testing habits (pytest) and code quality practices (Black, Ruff, type hints) — these signal engineering maturity above what most junior candidates demonstrate.

faq.question: What is the difference between a Python developer and a software engineer resume?
faq.answer: A Python developer resume leads with Python-specific frameworks and domain tools. A software engineer resume is more language-agnostic, emphasising system design, architecture, and cross-language experience. For Python-specific job postings, use 'Python Developer' or 'Python Engineer' in your summary and lead with Python frameworks. For broader engineering roles, emphasise architectural decisions and systems-level thinking alongside your Python work.

faq.question: Should Python developers include their test coverage on their resume?
faq.answer: Yes, if it is strong — 85%+ coverage is a meaningful signal. Mentioning your testing approach (pytest, factory_boy, Hypothesis for property-based testing) and the coverage percentage demonstrates engineering rigour that differentiates mid-level from senior candidates. If your team does not track coverage formally, reference your approach: 'wrote unit and integration tests for all business logic; maintained pre-commit hooks with Ruff and Black'.

faq.question: How do I list Python on a UK CV?
faq.answer: The same principles apply: lead with your domain (web, data, ML), list specific frameworks rather than just Python, and include quantified bullet examples. UK Python roles often list Django and Flask more than FastAPI (though FastAPI is growing). For data engineering roles in the UK, dbt + BigQuery/Snowflake and Airflow are the standard stack. UK employers in finance and healthtech particularly value GDPR-compliant data handling and SOC 2 / ISO 27001 awareness.

faq.question: How do I write Python resume bullets if I have no professional experience?
faq.answer: Use your personal projects and open-source contributions as experience. Structure each project bullet like a professional bullet: state what you built, the tech stack, and a measurable outcome. 'Built a FastAPI REST API with PostgreSQL backend, dockerised and deployed to Fly.io, serving 200 requests/day' is more effective than 'worked on a personal Python project'. Contributing to an open-source Python project — even small documentation fixes — is worth mentioning as it shows engagement with the ecosystem.

faq.question: What Python AI and LLM skills should I add to my resume?
faq.answer: The most in-demand LLM/AI Python skills in 2026: LangChain and LlamaIndex for RAG and agent pipelines; Hugging Face Transformers and PEFT/LoRA for fine-tuning; OpenAI and Anthropic SDK integration (API calls, function calling, tool use, streaming); Pinecone, Weaviate, or pgvector for vector databases; MLflow or Weights & Biases for experiment tracking; and asyncio-based streaming response handling. Even one shipped project using any of these is worth highlighting prominently.

commonMistakes: Listing 'Python' as the only programming language entry with no frameworks
Using domain-agnostic bullets ('worked on backend' vs 'built FastAPI service handling 8K RPM')
No scale or performance metrics on any project
Listing frameworks across multiple domains without indicating primary expertise
Omitting testing, type checking, or code quality signals — this is expected at senior level
Not mentioning async/await or concurrency experience for backend or data roles
Using years-of-experience framing instead of achievement bullets
Failing to tailor the skills section to each role's specific domain and stack

HR resumes should show both operational scope (headcount managed, roles filled, compliance maintained) and strategic impact (retention improvements, cost savings, culture initiatives). Use the specific HRIS tools and systems from the job description — recruiters filter by platform experience. Show progression from transactional HR tasks to strategic business partnership.

Tailoring your HR resume to each role is critical because HR spans many specializations. WadeCV can help you align your experience with specific job descriptions, whether the role emphasizes recruiting, compliance, people analytics, or organizational development.

faq.question: What skills should I put on an HR resume?
faq.answer: Include a mix of technical HR skills (HRIS platforms, ATS tools, payroll systems), functional skills (recruiting, benefits administration, compliance), and strategic skills (people analytics, workforce planning, DEI). Always match the specific tools and competencies mentioned in the job description.

faq.question: How do I show HR impact on a resume?
faq.answer: Quantify everything: roles filled, time-to-fill, retention rates, cost savings from benefits negotiations, training completion rates, employee satisfaction scores. Frame results in terms of business impact, not just HR activities.

faq.question: Should I list HRIS systems on my resume?
faq.answer: Yes. Recruiters and ATS systems filter by specific platforms (Workday, BambooHR, ADP, SAP SuccessFactors). List every HRIS, ATS, and payroll system you have used in a dedicated Skills or Tools section.
